title: Sliding Barn Door
summary: A Z-braced plank door with pocket-hole frame joinery and a plywood back — build to your exact opening, then hang on sliding track hardware.
description:
This is the classic Z-brace barn door look: a solid frame with a diagonal brace running corner to corner, backed with plywood so it reads as a solid slab from the front. The Z-brace isn't purely decorative — those angled pieces triangulate the frame and keep a door this size from racking into a parallelogram over years of sliding back and forth, which a plain rectangular frame would eventually do.

Measure your actual opening before cutting anything. A barn door needs to overlap the opening by several inches on both sides and the top so it fully covers the gap when closed, and that overlap comes straight out of your frame dimensions — there's no universal size here. Cut the two angled brace pieces last, holding them in place across the assembled frame and marking the angles directly off the real geometry, rather than trusting an angle number that assumes your frame came out exactly on paper.
timeLabel: 4–6 hrs for the door, plus mounting the track hardware

tools.note: Frame joints are pocket screws so nothing shows on the door's face.
For hanging the track and checking the door hangs plumb.

materials.name: 1x3 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Stiles and the two angled Z-brace pieces.

materials.name: 1/2" exterior plywood, cut to door size
materials.note: Backs the frame so the door reads as a solid slab from the front.

materials.name: Sliding barn door track hardware kit
materials.note: Buy a kit rated for your door's actual weight and width — this is what makes it a barn door rather than a hinged one.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 25° off square, ends parallel to each other. Mark and cut off the assembled frame, not from this number alone.
One end at 40° off square, other end at 25° off square, cut in opposite directions.

steps.title: Measure your opening and confirm sizes
steps.body: Measure the actual width and height of your door opening, then size the finished door to overlap it by at least 2" on each side and the top so it fully covers the gap when closed. Adjust every rail and stile length in the cut list to match your real opening before cutting — the numbers here assume a specific opening and yours is almost certainly different.

steps.title: Build the frame
steps.body: Drill pocket holes in the ends of the top, mid, and bottom rails and join them to the two stiles with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue, keeping everything square. This front-facing frame is what you'll see once the door is hung, so keep glue squeeze-out to a minimum on the front face.

steps.title: Cut and fit the Z-brace
steps.body: Hold the long diagonal brace across the assembled frame corner to corner and mark the actual angles directly off the frame, rather than trusting the reference angle alone — your real frame's exact width and height determine the true angle. Cut and fit it, then do the same for the two shorter diagonals that complete the Z. Pocket-screw and glue all three in place.

steps.title: Attach the back frame and plywood
steps.body: Screw the two back frame rails and the cross rail to the back face of the door, then attach the plywood panel over them with glue and screws driven from the back only, so nothing shows on the front. This is what gives the door its solid-slab look from the room side.
materials: 1/2" exterior plywood, cut to door size

steps.title: Sand, finish, and hang
steps.body: Sand every edge and both faces, finish as you like, then mount the track hardware to your wall header per the kit's instructions and hang the door. Check it slides smoothly and hangs plumb before calling it done — most sliding-door binding issues trace back to the header not being level.
materials: Sliding barn door track hardware kit

images.alt: Z-braced sliding barn door with a plywood back panel
